mn56:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Nāḷandā in Pāvārika’s Mango Grove.
mn56:10.1: When this was said, the householder Upāli said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn56:10.3: Just as might be expected from a learned disciple who properly understands the teacher’s instruction, so indeed it was well explained by Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha to the *Bhagavā*.
mn56:10.4: For what can that wretched mental harm amount to in comparison with this gross bodily harm?
mn56:10.5: Surely bodily harm alone is more gravely blameworthy for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*, not so verbal harm, not so mental harm.”
mn56:10.6: “If, householder, you would discuss while grounded in truth, there could be a discussion here.”
mn56:10.7: “I will discuss while grounded in truth, *bhante*.
mn56:10.8: Let there be a discussion here.”
mn56:11.10: For this statement was spoken by you, householder:
mn56:11.11: ‘I will discuss while grounded in truth, *bhante*.
mn56:11.12: Let there be a discussion here.’”
mn56:11.13: “Even though the *Bhagavā* says so, still bodily harm alone is more gravely blameworthy for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*, not so verbal harm, not so mental harm.”
mn56:11.2: Here a Nigaṇṭha is ill, afflicted, seriously sick, one who rejects cold water and resorts to hot water.
mn56:11.3: Not getting cold water, he might die.
mn56:11.4: Now in what destination does Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta declare that Nigaṇṭha to be reborn?”
mn56:11.5: “There are *devatās* called Mind-Beings, *bhante*; there he is reborn.”
mn56:11.7: “Because, *bhante*, he dies mentally attached to it.”
mn56:11.8: “Attend carefully, householder. Having attended carefully, answer.
mn56:11.9: What you said later does not fit with what you said earlier, nor what you said earlier with what you said later.
mn56:12.10: “Attend carefully, householder. Having attended carefully, answer.
mn56:12.11: What you said later does not fit with what you said earlier, nor what you said earlier with what you said later.
mn56:12.12: For this statement was spoken by you, householder:
mn56:12.13: ‘I will discuss while grounded in truth, *bhante*;
mn56:12.14: let there be a discussion here.’”
mn56:12.15: “Even though the *Bhagavā* says so, still bodily harm alone is more gravely blameworthy for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*, not so verbal harm, not so mental harm.”
mn56:12.2: Here a Nigaṇṭha, according to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, is enclosed by restraint in the fourfold control, barred in every respect, bound in every respect, shaken free in every respect, and pervaded in every respect.
mn56:12.3: While walking forward and backward he causes the destruction of many tiny living beings.
mn56:12.4: Now what result does Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta declare for him?”
mn56:12.5: “Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, *bhante*, does not declare what is unintentional to be greatly blameworthy.”
mn56:12.8: “And in which does Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta place intention, householder?”
mn56:13.10: “Not even ten men, *bhante*, not even twenty men, not even thirty men, not even forty men, not even fifty men would be able in one moment, in one instant, to make all the living beings in this Nāḷandā into one lump of flesh, one heap of flesh.
mn56:13.13: suppose an ascetic or brahmin were to come here possessing supernormal power, having attained mastery over the mind.
mn56:13.15: ‘With a single malicious thought I will turn this Nāḷandā to ashes.’
mn56:13.17: would that ascetic or brahmin, possessing supernormal power and having attained mastery over the mind, be able with a single malicious thought to turn this Nāḷandā to ashes?”
mn56:13.18: “Ten Nāḷandās, *bhante*, twenty Nāḷandās, thirty Nāḷandās, forty Nāḷandās, fifty Nāḷandās — that ascetic or brahmin, possessing supernormal power and having attained mastery over the mind, would be able to turn them to ashes with a single malicious thought.
mn56:13.2: is this Nāḷandā prosperous and flourishing, crowded with many people?”
mn56:13.20: “Attend carefully, householder. Having attended carefully, answer.
mn56:13.21: What you said later does not fit with what you said earlier, nor what you said earlier with what you said later.
mn56:13.22: For this statement was spoken by you, householder:
mn56:13.23: ‘I will discuss while grounded in truth, *bhante*;
mn56:13.24: let there be a discussion here.’”
mn56:13.25: “Even though the *Bhagavā* says so, still bodily harm alone is more gravely blameworthy for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*, not so verbal harm, not so mental harm.”
mn56:13.27: have you heard that the Dandaka forest, the Kāliṅga forest, the Majjha forest, and the Mātaṅga forest became wild forest?”
mn56:13.28: “Yes, *bhante*, I have heard that the Dandaka forest, the Kāliṅga forest, the Majjha forest, and the Mātaṅga forest became wild forest.”
mn56:13.3: “Yes, *bhante*, this Nāḷandā is prosperous and flourishing, crowded with many people.”
mn56:13.5: suppose a man were to come here with sword upraised.
mn56:13.7: ‘In one moment, in one instant, I will make all the living beings in this Nāḷandā into one lump of flesh, one heap of flesh.’
mn56:13.9: would that man be able in one moment, in one instant, to make all the living beings in this Nāḷandā into one lump of flesh, one heap of flesh?”
mn56:14.1: “What do you think, householder, how have you heard it, by what did the Dandaka forest, the Kāliṅga forest, the Majjha forest, and the Mātaṅga forest become wild forest?”
mn56:14.2: “I have heard, *bhante*, that by the malicious thought of seers, the Dandaka forest, the Kāliṅga forest, the Majjha forest, and the Mātaṅga forest became wild forest.”
mn56:14.3: “Attend carefully, householder. Having attended carefully, answer.
mn56:14.4: What you said later does not fit with what you said earlier, nor what you said earlier with what you said later.
mn56:14.5: For this statement was spoken by you, householder:
mn56:14.6: ‘I will discuss while grounded in truth, *bhante*;
mn56:14.7: let there be a discussion here.’”
mn56:15.1: “Already with the first simile, *bhante*, I was pleased and delighted with the *Bhagavā*.
mn56:15.2: But I was wishing to hear these remarkable inspired answers to questions from the *Bhagavā*, and so I thought it right to take an opposing stance toward the *Bhagavā*.
mn56:15.4: Just as if someone were to set upright what had been overturned, or reveal what was concealed, or point out the way to one who was lost, or hold up an oil lamp in the dark so that those with eyes could see forms — in the same way the *Bhagavā* has made the *dhamma* clear in many ways.
mn56:15.5: I go for refuge to the *Bhagavā*, to the *dhamma*, and to the *bhikkhu saṅgha* [*bhikkhusaṅghañca*].
mn56:15.6: May the *Bhagavā* remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day on for life.”
mn56:16.1: “Make a thorough examination, householder. Thorough examination is good for well-known people such as you.”
mn56:16.2: “By this too, *bhante*, I am even more pleased and delighted with the *Bhagavā*, in that the *Bhagavā* says this to me: ‘Make a thorough examination, householder. Thorough examination is good for well-known people such as you.’
mn56:16.3: For if followers of other sects had gained a disciple like me, they would carry a banner all around Nāḷandā proclaiming:
mn56:16.4: ‘The householder Upāli has become our disciple.’
mn56:16.5: Yet the *Bhagavā* says this to me:
mn56:16.6: ‘Make a thorough examination, householder. Thorough examination is good for well-known people such as you.’
mn56:16.7: For a second time, *bhante*, I go for refuge to the *Bhagavā*, to the *dhamma*, and to the *bhikkhu saṅgha* [*bhikkhusaṅghañca*].
mn56:16.8: May the *Bhagavā* remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day on for life.”
mn56:17.1: “For a long time, householder, your family has been like a watering place for the Nigaṇṭhas, so you should consider that almsfood ought to be given to them when they come.”
mn56:17.10: For a third time, *bhante*, I go for refuge to the *Bhagavā*, to the *dhamma*, and to the *bhikkhu saṅgha* [*bhikkhusaṅghañca*].
mn56:17.11: May the *Bhagavā* remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day on for life.”
mn56:17.2: “By this too, *bhante*, I am even more pleased and delighted with the *Bhagavā*, in that the *Bhagavā* says this to me: ‘For a long time, householder, your family has been like a watering place for the Nigaṇṭhas, so you should consider that almsfood ought to be given to them when they come.’
mn56:17.3: I have heard, *bhante*, that the ascetic Gotama says this:
mn56:17.4: ‘Gifts should be given only to me, not gifts to others;
mn56:17.5: gifts should be given only to my disciples, not gifts to the disciples of others;
mn56:17.6: what is given only to me is of great fruit, what is given to others is not of great fruit;
mn56:17.7: what is given only to my disciples is of great fruit, what is given to the disciples of others is not of great fruit.’
mn56:17.8: Yet the *Bhagavā* encourages me to give even to the Nigaṇṭhas.
mn56:17.9: Still, *bhante*, we shall know the proper time for that.
mn56:18.1: Then the *Bhagavā* gave the householder Upāli a gradual talk, that is to say—
mn56:18.10: “Now is the time for you, householder, to do as you think fit.”
mn56:18.2: he spoke on giving, on ethical conduct, on heaven; he revealed the drawback, degradation, and defilement in sensual pleasures, and the benefit in renunciation.
mn56:18.3: When the *Bhagavā* knew that the householder Upāli had a ready mind, a pliant mind, a mind free of hindrances, an uplifted mind, a serene mind, then he revealed the teaching of the Buddhas that is supreme:
mn56:18.5: Just as a clean cloth, free of stains, would properly take dye,
mn56:18.6: in the same way, while still seated there, the stainless, dust-free *dhamma*-eye [*dhammacakkhuṁ*] arose in the householder Upāli:
mn56:18.7: “Whatever is of a nature to arise, all that is of a nature to cease.”
mn56:18.8: Then the householder Upāli, having seen the *dhamma* [*diṭṭhadhammo*], attained the *dhamma* [*pattadhammo*], known the *dhamma* [*viditadhammo*], and plunged into the *dhamma* [*pariyogāḷhadhammo*], having crossed beyond doubt, gone beyond perplexity, attained confidence, and become independent of others in the Teacher’s instruction, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn56:18.9: “Come then now, *bhante*, we will go. We have much work, much to do.”
mn56:19.1: Then the householder Upāli, having delighted in and approved of what the *Bhagavā* had said, rose from his seat, paid homage to the *Bhagavā*, circumambulated him keeping him on his right, and went to his own residence. Having gone there, he addressed the gatekeeper:
mn56:19.2: “From this day on, good gatekeeper, I close the door to the Nigaṇṭhas and Nigaṇṭhīs, and open the door to the *Bhagavā*, to the *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhunis*, laymen, and laywomen.
mn56:19.3: If any Nigaṇṭha comes, you should say this to him:
mn56:19.5: From this day on the householder Upāli has become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.
mn56:19.6: The door is closed to the Nigaṇṭhas and Nigaṇṭhīs, and open to the *Bhagavā*, to the *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhunis*, laymen, and laywomen.
mn56:19.7: If you need almsfood, *bhante*, stand right there; they will bring it for you right there.’”
mn56:19.8: “Yes, *bhante*,” the gatekeeper replied to the householder Upāli.
mn56:2.1: Now at that time Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta was residing at Nāḷandā together with a large assembly of Nigaṇṭhas.
mn56:2.2: Then Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha, after wandering for alms in Nāḷandā and after the meal, when he had returned from his almsround, went to Pāvārika’s Mango Grove, to the *Bhagavā*.
mn56:2.3: Having gone up, he exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā*. After exchanging courteous and memorable talk, he stood to one side. Standing to one side, Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha, the *Bhagavā* said this:
mn56:2.4: “There are seats, Tapassi. If you wish, sit down.”
mn56:20.11: that the ascetic Gotama should become a disciple of the householder Upāli.”
mn56:20.12: “Come then, *bhante*, I will go and find out whether or not the householder Upāli has become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.”
mn56:20.13: “Go then, Tapassi, and find out whether or not the householder Upāli has become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.”
mn56:20.2: “They say the householder Upāli has become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.”
mn56:20.3: Then Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha went to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, and having gone up, said this to him:
mn56:20.4: “I have heard, *bhante*, that they say the householder Upāli has become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.”
mn56:20.5: “That is impossible, Tapassi, it cannot happen that the householder Upāli should become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.
mn56:20.6: But it is possible that the ascetic Gotama should become a disciple of the householder Upāli.”
mn56:20.7: A second time Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha …
mn56:20.8: A third time Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha said this to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta:
mn56:21.1: Then Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha went to the residence of the householder Upāli.
mn56:21.10: This was what I did not want from you, *bhante*; it did not please me that the householder Upāli should charge the ascetic Gotama with a thesis.
mn56:21.11: For the ascetic Gotama, *bhante*, is a magician; he knows a converting magic by which he converts the disciples of other sects.
mn56:21.12: The householder Upāli has been converted by the ascetic Gotama with that converting magic.”
mn56:21.13: “That is impossible, Tapassi, it cannot happen that the householder Upāli should become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.
mn56:21.14: But it is possible that the ascetic Gotama should become a disciple of the householder Upāli.”
mn56:21.15: A second time Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha said this to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta:
mn56:21.17: that the ascetic Gotama should become a disciple of the householder Upāli.”
mn56:21.18: A third time Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha said this to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta:
mn56:21.2: The gatekeeper saw Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha coming in the distance.
mn56:21.21: that the ascetic Gotama should become a disciple of the householder Upāli.”
mn56:21.22: “Come then, Tapassi, I will go and find out for myself whether or not the householder Upāli has become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.”
mn56:21.3: Seeing him, he said this to Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha:
mn56:21.5: From this day on the householder Upāli has become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.
mn56:21.6: The door is closed to the Nigaṇṭhas and Nigaṇṭhīs, and open to the *Bhagavā*, to the *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhunis*, laymen, and laywomen.
mn56:21.7: If you need almsfood, *bhante*, stand right there; they will bring it for you right there.”
mn56:21.8: Saying, “I have no need of almsfood, friend,” he turned back from there and went to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta. Having gone up, he said this to him:
mn56:21.9: “It is really true, *bhante*, that the householder Upāli has become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.
mn56:22.1: Then Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta together with a large assembly of Nigaṇṭhas went to the residence of the householder Upāli.
mn56:22.11: “Yes, *bhante*,” the gatekeeper replied to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta. Then he went to the householder Upāli, and having gone up, said this to him:
mn56:22.12: “Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, *bhante*, together with a large assembly of Nigaṇṭhas, is standing in the outer gatehouse.
mn56:22.14: “Then, good gatekeeper, set out seats in the middle gatehall.”
mn56:22.15: “Yes, *bhante*,” the gatekeeper replied to the householder Upāli. Then, having set out seats in the middle gatehall, he went to the householder Upāli and, having gone up, said this to him:
mn56:22.16: “The seats have been set out in the middle gatehall, *bhante*.
mn56:22.17: Now is the time for you to do as you think fit.”
mn56:22.2: The gatekeeper saw Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta coming in the distance.
mn56:22.3: Seeing him, he said this to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta:
mn56:22.5: From this day on the householder Upāli has become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.
mn56:22.6: The door is closed to the Nigaṇṭhas and Nigaṇṭhīs, and open to the *Bhagavā*, to the *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhunis*, laymen, and laywomen.
mn56:22.7: If you need almsfood, *bhante*, stand right there; they will bring it for you right there.”
mn56:22.8: “Then, good gatekeeper, go to the householder Upāli. Having gone up, say this to the householder Upāli:
mn56:22.9: ‘Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, *bhante*, together with a large assembly of Nigaṇṭhas, is standing in the outer gatehouse.
mn56:23.1: Then the householder Upāli went to the middle gatehall. Having gone there, he sat himself down on the seat there that was foremost, best, highest, and finest, and addressed the gatekeeper:
mn56:23.2: “Then, good gatekeeper, go to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta. Having gone up, say this to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta:
mn56:23.5: “Yes, *bhante*,” the gatekeeper replied to the householder Upāli. Then he went to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta and, having gone up, said this to him:
mn56:23.8: Then Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta together with a large assembly of Nigaṇṭhas went to the middle gatehall.
mn56:24.2: whereas formerly, whenever he saw Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta coming in the distance, he would go out to meet him, wipe with his upper robe the seat there that was foremost, best, highest, and finest, arrange it, and have him sit down on it—
mn56:24.3: now, having himself sat down on the seat there that was foremost, best, highest, and finest, said this to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta:
mn56:25.1: When this was said, Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta said this to the householder Upāli:
mn56:25.2: “You are mad, householder, you are done for, householder.
mn56:25.3: You went saying, ‘I will go, *bhante*, and charge the ascetic Gotama with a thesis,’ and you have come back struck head-on by a massive blow in debate.
mn56:25.4: Just as a man might go to one who removes testicles and come back with his testicles removed, or just as a man might go to one who removes eyes and come back with his eyes removed;
mn56:25.5: so too, householder, you went saying, ‘I will go, *bhante*, and charge the ascetic Gotama with a thesis,’ and you have come back struck head-on by a massive blow in debate.
mn56:25.6: You have been converted by the ascetic Gotama with that converting magic, householder.”
mn56:26.3: if my dear relatives and blood-kin were converted by that converting magic, it would be for their long-term welfare and happiness;
mn56:26.4: if all nobles, *bhante*, were converted by that converting magic, it would be for the long-term welfare and happiness of them all; if all brahmins … merchants … workers were converted by that converting magic, it would be for the long-term welfare and happiness of them all;
mn56:26.5: if even the world with its *devatās*, Māra, and Brahmā, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, with its *devatās* and humans, were converted by that converting magic, it would be for the long-term welfare and happiness of the whole world with its *devatās*, Māra, and Brahmā, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, with its *devatās* and humans.
mn56:26.6: Well then, *bhante*, I will make a simile for you.
mn56:26.7: For some wise people here understand the meaning of what is said by means of a simile.
mn56:27.1: “Formerly, *bhante*, a certain brahmin, old, elderly, advanced in years, had a young brahmin wife who was pregnant and close to giving birth.
mn56:27.10: ‘Go, brahmin, buy a baby monkey from the market and bring it here. It will be a plaything for my little boy.’
mn56:27.11: Then, *bhante*, that brahmin, infatuated with that young woman and with his mind attached to her, bought a baby monkey from the market and brought it, and said this to that young woman:
mn56:27.12: ‘Here, lady, a baby monkey has been bought from the market and brought for you; it will be a plaything for your little boy.’
mn56:27.13: When this was said, *bhante*, that young woman said this to that brahmin:
mn56:27.14: ‘Go, brahmin, take this baby monkey and go to Rattapāṇi the dyer’s son. Having gone up, say this to Rattapāṇi the dyer’s son—
mn56:27.15: “Friend Rattapāṇi, I want this baby monkey dyed in the color called yellow-application, beaten and beaten back, and rubbed on both sides.”’
mn56:27.16: Then, *bhante*, that brahmin, infatuated with that young woman and with his mind attached to her, took that baby monkey and went to Rattapāṇi the dyer’s son. Having gone up, he said this to Rattapāṇi the dyer’s son:
mn56:27.17: ‘Friend Rattapāṇi, I want this baby monkey dyed in the color called yellow-application, beaten and beaten back, and rubbed on both sides.’
mn56:27.18: When this was said, *bhante*, Rattapāṇi the dyer’s son said this to that brahmin:
mn56:27.19: ‘This baby monkey of yours, *bhante*, is fit for dyeing, but not fit for beating, not fit for rubbing.’
mn56:27.2: Then that young brahmin woman said this to that brahmin, *bhante*:
mn56:27.20: Just so, *bhante*, the doctrine of foolish Nigaṇṭhas is fit for dyeing only among fools, not among the wise; it is not fit for examination, not fit for rubbing clean.
mn56:27.21: Then, *bhante*, at a later time that brahmin took a new pair of cloths and went to Rattapāṇi the dyer’s son. Having gone up, he said this to Rattapāṇi the dyer’s son:
mn56:27.22: ‘Friend Rattapāṇi, I want this new pair of cloths dyed in the color called yellow-application, beaten and beaten back, and rubbed on both sides.’
mn56:27.23: When this was said, *bhante*, Rattapāṇi the dyer’s son said this to that brahmin:
mn56:27.24: ‘This new pair of cloths of yours, *bhante*, is fit for dyeing, fit for beating, and fit for rubbing.’
mn56:27.25: Just so, *bhante*, the doctrine of that *Bhagavā*, the *arahant*, the perfectly awakened one, is fit for dyeing among the wise and not among fools; it is fit for examination and fit for rubbing clean.”
mn56:27.3: ‘Go, brahmin, buy a baby monkey from the market and bring it here. It will be a plaything for my little boy.’
mn56:27.4: When this was said, that brahmin said this to that young woman:
mn56:27.5: ‘Just wait, lady, until you give birth.
mn56:27.6: If you give birth to a little boy, I will buy a baby monkey from the market and bring it for him, and it will be a plaything for your little boy.
mn56:27.7: But if you give birth to a little girl, I will buy a baby she-monkey from the market and bring it for her, and it will be a plaything for your little girl.’
mn56:27.8: A second time, *bhante*, that young woman …
mn56:27.9: A third time, *bhante*, that young woman said this to that brahmin:
mn56:28.1: “The assembly together with its leaders understands thus:
mn56:28.2: ‘The householder Upāli is a disciple of Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta.’
mn56:28.3: Householder, whose disciple shall we regard you as?”
mn56:29.1: When this was said, the householder Upāli rose from his seat, arranged his upper robe over one shoulder, raised his joined palms toward the *Bhagavā*, and said this to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta:
mn56:29.10: who has vomited up worldly bait, joyful;
mn56:29.11: of the human who has fulfilled the ascetic life,
mn56:29.12: the man in his final body;
mn56:29.14: of that *Bhagavā* I am a disciple.
mn56:29.15: Of the one without uncertainty, skillful,
mn56:29.19: the hero who cuts off conceit—
mn56:29.20: of that *Bhagavā* I am a disciple.
mn56:29.22: deep, one who has attained silence;
mn56:29.26: of that *Bhagavā* I am a disciple.
mn56:29.27: Of the nāga, with a secluded army,
mn56:29.3: Of the steadfast one, free from delusion,
mn56:29.30: with banner cast down, free of passion;
mn56:29.32: of that *Bhagavā* I am a disciple.
mn56:29.33: Of the best of seers, without deceit,
mn56:29.34: possessed of the three knowledges, one who has reached Brahmā;
mn56:29.35: bathed, one who has reached the goal,
mn56:29.37: the bountiful giver, the able one—
mn56:29.38: of that *Bhagavā* I am a disciple.
mn56:29.39: Of the noble one, with self developed [*bhāvitattassa*],
mn56:29.4: whose stakes are split apart, who has conquered conquest;
mn56:29.40: accomplished in attainment and in explanation;
mn56:29.42: not bent forward, not drawn back;
mn56:29.44: of that *Bhagavā* I am a disciple.
mn56:29.46: with inner being not led along, pure;
mn56:29.48: secluded [*pavivittassa*], who has reached the highest;
mn56:29.49: crossed over, and one who carries others across—
mn56:29.5: free from distress, with a very well-balanced mind,
mn56:29.50: of that *Bhagavā* I am a disciple.
mn56:29.51: Of the peaceful one, of abundant discernment [*paññā*],
mn56:29.52: of great discernment [*paññā*], free of greed;
mn56:29.56: of that *Bhagavā* I am a disciple.
mn56:29.57: Of the awakened one who cuts off *taṇhā*,
mn56:29.59: worthy of offerings, a powerful being,
mn56:29.6: mature in ethical conduct, of good discernment [*paññā*];
mn56:29.61: great, who has reached the summit of renown—
mn56:29.62: of that *Bhagavā* I am a disciple.”
mn56:29.8: of that *Bhagavā* I am a disciple.
mn56:29.9: Of the one without doubt, content,
mn56:3.1: When this was said, Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha took a certain low seat and sat down to one side.
mn56:3.10: “Bodily harm is one thing, friend Gotama, verbal harm another, and mental harm another.”
mn56:3.11: “But of these three kinds of harm, thus distinguished and thus differentiated, which kind of harm does Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta declare to be more gravely blameworthy for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*: bodily harm, verbal harm, or mental harm?”
mn56:3.12: “Of these three kinds of harm, friend Gotama, thus distinguished and thus differentiated, Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta declares bodily harm to be more gravely blameworthy for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*, not so verbal harm, not so mental harm.”
mn56:3.13: “Is it bodily harm, Tapassi, that you say?”
mn56:3.14: “It is bodily harm, friend Gotama, that I say.”
mn56:3.15: “Is it bodily harm, Tapassi, that you say?”
mn56:3.16: “It is bodily harm, friend Gotama, that I say.”
mn56:3.17: “Is it bodily harm, Tapassi, that you say?”
mn56:3.18: “It is bodily harm, friend Gotama, that I say.”
mn56:3.19: So the *Bhagavā* got Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha to affirm this point under discussion up to a third time.
mn56:3.2: When Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha was sitting to one side, the *Bhagavā* said this to him:
mn56:3.3: “But how many kinds of harm, Tapassi, does Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta lay down for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*?”
mn56:3.4: “It is not the custom of Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, friend Gotama, to lay it down as ‘*kamma*, *kamma*.’
mn56:3.5: Rather, friend Gotama, it is the custom of Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta to lay it down as ‘harm, harm.’”
mn56:3.6: “But how many kinds of harm, Tapassi, does Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta lay down for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*?”
mn56:3.7: “Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, friend Gotama, lays down three kinds of harm for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*. That is to say—
mn56:3.8: bodily harm, verbal harm, and mental harm.”
mn56:3.9: “But, Tapassi, is bodily harm one thing, verbal harm another, and mental harm another?”
mn56:30.1: “But when were these praises of the ascetic Gotama composed by you, householder?”
mn56:30.2: “Just as from a great heap of many kinds of flowers, a skilled garland-maker or a garland-maker’s apprentice might weave a variegated garland,
mn56:30.3: even so, *bhante*, that *Bhagavā* is of many excellences, of many hundreds of excellences.
mn56:30.4: For who would not praise one worthy of praise?”
mn56:31.1: Then Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, unable to endure the honor shown to the *Bhagavā*, right there brought up hot blood from his mouth.
mn56:31.2: The Upāli sutta is finished, the sixth.
mn56:4.1: When this was said, Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn56:4.10: “But of these three kinds of *kamma*, thus distinguished and thus differentiated, which *kamma* do you declare to be more gravely blameworthy for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*: bodily *kamma* [*kāyakammaṁ*], verbal *kamma* [*vacīkammaṁ*], or mental *kamma* [*manokammaṁ*]?”
mn56:4.11: “Of these three kinds of *kamma*, Tapassi, thus distinguished and thus differentiated, I declare mental *kamma* [*manokammaṁ*] to be more gravely blameworthy for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*, not so bodily *kamma* [*kāyakammaṁ*], not so verbal *kamma* [*vacīkammaṁ*].”
mn56:4.12: “Is it mental *kamma* [*manokammaṁ*], friend Gotama, that you say?”
mn56:4.13: “It is mental *kamma* [*manokammaṁ*], Tapassi, that I say.”
mn56:4.14: “Is it mental *kamma* [*manokammaṁ*], friend Gotama, that you say?”
mn56:4.15: “It is mental *kamma* [*manokammaṁ*], Tapassi, that I say.”
mn56:4.16: “Is it mental *kamma* [*manokammaṁ*], friend Gotama, that you say?”
mn56:4.17: “It is mental *kamma* [*manokammaṁ*], Tapassi, that I say.”
mn56:4.18: So Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha, having got the *Bhagavā* to affirm this point under discussion up to a third time, rose from his seat and went to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta.
mn56:4.2: “But you, friend Gotama, how many kinds of harm do you lay down for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*?”
mn56:4.3: “It is not the custom of the Tathāgata, Tapassi, to lay it down as ‘harm, harm.’
mn56:4.4: Rather, Tapassi, it is the custom of the Tathāgata to lay it down as ‘*kamma*, *kamma*.’”
mn56:4.5: “But you, friend Gotama, how many kinds of *kamma* do you lay down for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*?”
mn56:4.6: “I, Tapassi, lay down three kinds of *kamma* for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*. That is to say—
mn56:4.8: “But, friend Gotama, is bodily *kamma* [*kāyakammaṁ*] one thing, verbal *kamma* [*vacīkammaṁ*] another, and mental *kamma* [*manokammaṁ*] another?”
mn56:4.9: “Bodily *kamma* [*kāyakammaṁ*] is one thing, Tapassi, verbal *kamma* [*vacīkammaṁ*] another, and mental *kamma* [*manokammaṁ*] another.”
mn56:5.1: Now at that time Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta was sitting with a large assembly of householders, a childish crowd headed by Upāli.
mn56:5.2: Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta saw Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha coming in the distance.
mn56:5.3: Seeing him, he said this to Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha:
mn56:5.4: “Well then, Tapassi, where are you coming from in the middle of the day?”
mn56:5.5: “I am coming from the presence of the ascetic Gotama, *bhante*.”
mn56:5.6: “But was there some discussion between you and the ascetic Gotama?”
mn56:5.7: “There was some discussion between me and the ascetic Gotama, *bhante*.”
mn56:5.8: “And what was the discussion between you and the ascetic Gotama, Tapassi?”
mn56:5.9: Then Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha reported to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta all the discussion that had taken place with the *Bhagavā*.
mn56:6.1: When this was said, Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta said this to Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha:
mn56:6.3: Just as might be expected from a learned disciple who properly understands the teacher’s instruction, so indeed it was well explained by Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha to the ascetic Gotama.
mn56:6.4: For what can that wretched mental harm amount to in comparison with this gross bodily harm?
mn56:6.5: Surely bodily harm alone is more gravely blameworthy for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*, not so verbal harm, not so mental harm.”
mn56:7.1: When this was said, the householder Upāli said this to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta:
mn56:7.10: Just as a sixty-year-old elephant, plunging into a deep lotus pond, might play the sort of game called hemp-washing, so I think I will play with the ascetic Gotama as with hemp-washing play.
mn56:7.11: Come now, *bhante*, I will go and charge the ascetic Gotama with a thesis on this point under discussion.”
mn56:7.12: “Go then, householder, and charge the ascetic Gotama with a thesis on this point under discussion.
mn56:7.13: For I might charge the ascetic Gotama with a thesis, or Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha, or you.”
mn56:7.3: Just as might be expected from a learned disciple who properly understands the teacher’s instruction, so indeed it was well explained by the venerable Tapassi to the ascetic Gotama.
mn56:7.4: For what can that wretched mental harm amount to in comparison with this gross bodily harm?
mn56:7.5: Surely bodily harm alone is more gravely blameworthy for the doing of bad *kamma* and the carrying out of bad *kamma*, not so verbal harm, not so mental harm.
mn56:7.6: Come now, *bhante*, I will go and charge the ascetic Gotama with a thesis on this point under discussion.
mn56:7.7: If the ascetic Gotama stands the way he was made to stand by the venerable Tapassi, then just as a strong man might seize a long-fleeced ram by the fleece and drag it around, haul it around, and yank it all around, so I will drag the ascetic Gotama around in debate, haul him around, and yank him all around.
mn56:7.8: Just as a strong brewer’s workman, after throwing a great brewer’s wicker mat into a deep pool of water, might seize it by the edge and drag it around, haul it around, and yank it all around, so I will drag the ascetic Gotama around in debate, haul him around, and yank him all around.
mn56:7.9: Just as a strong brewer’s reveller might seize a hairy thing by the edge and shake it, jerk it, and snap it out, so I will shake the ascetic Gotama in debate, jerk him, and snap him out.
mn56:8.1: When this was said, Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha said this to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta:
mn56:8.2: “That does not please me, *bhante*, that the householder Upāli should charge the ascetic Gotama with a thesis.
mn56:8.3: For the ascetic Gotama, *bhante*, is a magician; he knows a converting magic by which he converts the disciples of other sects.”
mn56:8.4: “That is impossible, Tapassi, it cannot happen that the householder Upāli should become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.
mn56:8.5: But it is possible that the ascetic Gotama should become a disciple of the householder Upāli.
mn56:8.6: Go then, householder, and charge the ascetic Gotama with a thesis on this point under discussion.
mn56:8.7: For I might charge the ascetic Gotama with a thesis, or Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha, or you.”
mn56:9.11: “Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha did come here, householder.”
mn56:9.12: “But was there some discussion between you and Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha, *bhante*?”
mn56:9.13: “There was some discussion between me and Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha, householder.”
mn56:9.14: “And what was the discussion between you and Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha, *bhante*?”
mn56:9.15: Then the *Bhagavā* reported to the householder Upāli all the discussion that had taken place with Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha.
mn56:9.2: A third time Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha said this to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta:
mn56:9.3: “That does not please me, *bhante*, that the householder Upāli should charge the ascetic Gotama with a thesis.
mn56:9.4: For the ascetic Gotama, *bhante*, is a magician; he knows a converting magic by which he converts the disciples of other sects.”
mn56:9.5: “That is impossible, Tapassi, it cannot happen that the householder Upāli should become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.
mn56:9.6: But it is possible that the ascetic Gotama should become a disciple of the householder Upāli.
mn56:9.7: Go then, householder, and charge the ascetic Gotama with a thesis on this point under discussion.
mn56:9.8: For I might charge the ascetic Gotama with a thesis, or Dīghatapassī the Nigaṇṭha, or you.”
mn56:9.9: “Yes, *bhante*,” the householder Upāli replied to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta. Then he rose from his seat, paid homage to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, circumambulated him keeping him on his right, and went to Pāvārika’s Mango Grove, to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone up, he paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, the householder Upāli said this to the *Bhagavā*:
